Hackney Car Fares.

That the following be the Fares within the
Borough by set down :—

For a drive from any stand within the
Borough direct to any other stand or
place within the same, without any
delay, for not more than one person
and not exceeding 421b. luggage, Gd.

For more than one and not exceeding four

ersons with 84lb. luggage, is.

Between the hours of 10 o’clock at night and
g o'clock in the morning double fare.
For an hour by day, 28., and 18. for
everyhouror part ofan hourafterwards.

Hirer has the option, at conclusion of hiring,
to pay either by distance or time.

The affairs of the Hospital are managed by
a Committee of 13 members, called in the
Act of Parliament ** The Committee of
Management.” This Committee is elected
by the Governors from among their own
number, at their Annual General Meet-
ing, held on the second Monday in
Januvary in each year. No one but a
Governor is eligible to be elected on this
Committee— 11th George IV., chap. 72.

ADRMISSION OF PATIENTS.

Govenors or Subscribers wishing to have
a patient admitted to the house should give
him or her, as the case may be, the
necessary order of admission to the Resident
Medical Officer, Barrington’s Hospital, and
if there is a bed vacant, and the case is a
suitable one for treatment, the patient will
be received.

Orders of admission must be made on the
printed form, which may be had on applica-
tion to the Registrar.

Urgent cases are taken in at all hours of
the day or night, without an order of
admission.

The Dispensary is open all day and
night for the treatment of accidents.

NoTe—As the funds at the disposal of the
Governors are not large, and as the Institu-
tion was founded principally for the
treatment of accident cases,it is to be hoped
that the subscribers will recommend as
few Medical cases as they possibly can.

CALENDAR OF FAIRS OF MUNSTER FOR 1913.

To ensure the greatest possible accuracy, application is annually made by letter to the secretaries or proprietors of

all the Fairs published as held in Munster,

For the district it covers it may therefore be relied on as an authentic

list. Subscribers anxious to see the names and dates of their local fairs properly inserted are requested to furnish

information to the publishers from time to time.

Fairs held on dates of the month which fall on Sunday are sometimes held on the
and at other times (and chiefly so) on the following Monday.

LANDLORD AND TENANT.

In taking a house, even if no other
further agreement is drawn up, a perfectly
clear understanding should be come to a8
to the length of notice to be given, and
the time at which it shall be given: e.g..
it is of great importance whether three or
gix months' notice may be given at an
quarter, only to fall due at the correspond-
ing quarter day in the year to that upon
which the tenancy was commenced.

In an ordinary yearly tenancy, without
agreement to the contrary, six monthg’
notice must usually be given to fall due
on the same date in the year as the ten-
ancy commenced on. Notice to terminate
a tenmancy must be given at the proper
legal time, and must be definite, although
it may contain an offer to renew the ten-
ancy under different conditions. If the
notice is not in order it need not be re-
pudiated by the recipient, but can be

1 4'1'.: i I 1_:‘ -’._.. ‘__v . ‘_f:“.

‘;. :_,;-. "'.

' Statutes of Limitations, Shops Act, 1912. 43

simply ignored. It is advisable to let the
notice be in writing, as affording the
easiest means of proof. If possesslon i

- taken between two anarter days and rent

is paid for the odd time, the tenancy is
gaid to run from the next quarter day,
otherwise from the date of entry.

Deductione may not be made from the
rent for repairs done by the tenant on
the landlord’s behalf, neither does the
failure of the landlord to do proper re-
pairs form a ground for terminating the
tenancy, but action can be taken for
breach of contract. :

Under an agreement to keep premises in
reasonable renair it is generally conridered
necessary to paint outside every three
yveare and inside every seven, also to paper
avery seven years, under ordinary wear
and tear conditions. ? _

A landlord can distrain, without legal
process, for rent overdue, between sunriee
and sunset, but must not make forceful
entry. Lodger’s property is exempt if the
owner makes a proper declaration and
tenders any rent which he himself owes.

Tenants’ fixtures left on the premises
after the end of the tenancy are in law
given to the landlord and cannot be re
covered by the tenant.

A tenant or lessee may assgign his ten-
ancy unless the tenancy agreement or
lease forbids. If it does provide that he
is not to assign without consent, an assign-
ment without leave may cause a forfeiture
of the leassa.

STATUTES OF LIMITATIONS.

Debt.—The limitation, or lapse of time
which bars an action for a debt or cove-
nant, if founded upon a deed, is 20 years,
but if founded on any less formal agree-
ment, the limitation is 81X years. "

Land.—The limitation to recover land 1s
12 years from the time the right first
accrued.- This time also applies to the re-
covery of land by a morigagee, or one
who geeks to recover money chargeable
upon land. If a right to land accrues to a

erson, who is under such disability as
infancy, lunacy, &ec., the action may be
brought within six years of the ending of
such disability or of the disabled person's
death: but in no case of such disability
must the lapse of time be more than 30
yearsa.

Rent.—No action to recover arrears of
rent may be brought, except such arrears
have accrued within 6 years of the bring-
ing of the action. ; _ :

Formal Acknowledgment of liability will
render of no effect any lapse of time which
has occurred, but a fresh limitation com-
mences from the time of such acknow-

ledgment.
SHOPS ACT, 1912.

Applies in Ireland only to shops in Muni-
cipal boroughs, urban districts, and towns
which have Town Commissioners. Shop-
keepers in the smaller country towns and
villages and in rural districts are exempt.
All premises where any retail trade or
business is carried on are regarded as
ghops. Wholesale premises, as such. and
places subject to the Factory and Work-
ghops Acts, do not fall under the Shops

Act. The husiness of receiving orders for |

the manufacture of articles and the sale
and delivery of articles when made is
retail trade within tne meaning of the Act.
Barbers’ and hairdressers’ premises, public
houses, refreshment rooms, and auction
rooms (except where goods are sold by
aunction wholesale) are shops. Laundry re-
oeiving houses, servants’ registry offices,
and places where articles are let for hire

e not shops. Stalls in puoblic markets

stalls at railway stations for sale of books
are regarded as shops. Fairs and bazaars
and the sale of programmes and cata-
logues at theatres are excluded from the

The rates of sickness benefit and disable-
ment benefit are (in certain cases) lower:
for girls under 21, and the rate of sick-
ness benefit is lower for women over b0.

Persons under 21.

Sickness Benefit—538 a week for 13 weeks,.
4s a -week afterwards.

Disablement Benefit—4s a week.

The full rate will be payable when the

insured persons reach the age of 21.° If

they are married or have members of their
family wholly or mainly dependent upon

“them, they have a right to sickness and

disablement benefits at the full rate.

if a man receives board and lodging the
ordinary rate of 3d is deducted whatever
his wages. If he receives board or lodg-
ing, but not both, the value of the board
or lodging counts as part of his remunera-
tion. Remuneration means wages and the
value of other things a workman may
receive, for instances, meals.

If a woman receives board and lodging
the ordinary rate of 2d is deducted what-
ever her wages. If she receives board or
lodging, but not both, the value of the
board or lodging counts as part of her
remuneration. Remuneration means wages
and the value of other things a woman
may receive from her employer, for in-
stance, meals.

Special Rates of Contribution.

In certain classes of employment, if the
employer undertakes to pay full re-
muneration during the first six weeks of
sickness, a special rate of contributions
may be allowed in those cases where the
rate of remuneration is 10s a week or up-
wards.

The worker gets mno sickness benefit
during those six weeks, but has a right to
all the other benefits as usual.

Rate of remuneration per working day—

This does not mean the same thing as the
amount earned in any day; for instance,
if a man works for half a day only and
earns 1s 6d, his rate of remuneration ie
33 a day.

THE WORKMEN’S COMPENSATION AEY.
The  application of this Aect extends Q%
(2]

more than one-half of the industries
the country, and makes the employer o
labour liable to pay compensation for am
injury sustained in conseguence of, an
in the course of his employment, from
whatever caunse or accident which may
have arisen through such employment.
with the exception of those which are due
to the wilful misconduct of the injured
workman. _

No negligence is necessary on the part
of the workman or of his fellow employeex
to constitute, or to contribute to the lia-
bility. -

The amount of ecompensation to be
claimed is as follows:—

In case of death, if there are any de
pendants on the deceased, the amount of
three years’ wages, and this amount must
not be less than £150, nor more than £300.
But in case there are no such dependants.
the liability is confined to the payment for
medical attendance and funeral expenses,
such amount not being in excess of £10.

For an injury not ending fatally, the
employer is liable to a weekly payment
not exceeding fifty per cent. of the work-
man's average weeklv earnings for the
preceding twelve months, but not exceed-
ing £1 per week. These payments, after
running for six calendar months, may, by
agreement, be commuted by the payment
of 3{; lump sum, according to such agr®
ment,

Post Office Savings Bank.

Established at all Money Order Offices.
In ordinary cases the annual deposit must
not exceed £50, and the total amount £200.
No person can have more than one ac
count. Amounts placed in the bank on
behalf of children under seven years of
age cannot be withdrawn until the child
reaches that age, and then only under his
or her signature. -The smallest amount re-
ceived at a time is 18, and this may take
the form of 12 penny stamps affixed to a
special form. ach depositor receives a
bank book in which the amount of each
deposit should be entered at the time of
paying in. If the deposit iz £5 or over, a
special acknowledgment is sent from the
head Loudon office. Interest is allowed at
the rate of 23 per cent. Any amount up
to and including £1 may be withdrawn
without previous notice, on presenting the
bank book. The bank book should be sent
each year, postage free, on the anniver-
sary of the date when the account was
opened, to the Controller of the Savings
Bank Department for purposes of checking
and adding interest. The withdrawal of
-aoney can be expedited by telegraphing,
the depositor paying cost of telegrams.

No acknowledgment sent for any deposit
inder £5, the entry in the Depositor’s Book
eing conclugive evidence of the sum de-
posited.

Telegraphs.

The charge is 6d for the first twelve
words, including the address, and id. for
every additional word. Counting—compound
words, proper names, figures, abbrevia-
tions, &ec.

The cost of registering an abbreviated
address, which must consist of two words,
ia £1 1s.

Telegraph offices are usually open on
week-days from 8 a.m. to 8 p.m., but in
London and most of the largest provineial
towns, offices are always open, day and
night, and in many towns telegrams may
be despatched at any hour from the rail-
way etation.

Telegrams in plain language in any
modern European tongue are charged for
according to the number of words; but all
words not forming part of any modern
European language, and all unintelligible
combinations of letters are charged for at
the rate of five letters to a word.

Exceptions—Words which are ordinarily
written as one, or coupled by hyphens, as
"lIlGt-]}_EI‘-l[l-l&W,” “forty-seven,” “warehouse-
man,”’ are counted as one word.

Such names as O’'Neill, MacDonnell, and
names with the prefix "St.” are charged
for as single words, but double names like
Bouverie-Tracy, although written with a
hyphen, count as two words. Initial letters
are each counted as one word.

SBuch abbreviations as ‘“can’t,” “won’t,’”
“couldn’t” are counted as single words.

Figures are counted at the rate of five
figures to a word. For example, ‘38563
sounts as one word, while ““385634" counts
as two words. Names of towns and vil-
lages in the United Kingdom count as one
word. When words are underlined, or placed
in a parenthesis, or within inverted commasg,
one extra word is charged for.

One word each is also charged for the
ordinary signs of punctuation if they are
required to be transmitted.

When the address is beyond the limit of
free delivery, porterage is charged at the
rate of 3d per mile, or part of a mile, the
distance being calculated from the limit
of free delivery.

Telegrams (Forelgn and Colonial).
Must be written on special forms and
are accepted in the language of any of

the principal European countries. Ths
rates vary. See Official Postal Guide,

Postal and Telegraph Directory, Ireland.

HEAD OFFICES (printed in Capitals) transact all classes of Post Office business. Counfzes

indicated in italics.

(*) Money Order and Savings Bank Offices. Every money order office described also as a
telegraph office pay telegraph money orders, and with very few exceptions Postal Orders are
issued and paid at @/ Post Offices,

(2) Telegraph and Express Delivery Offices. The ordfnary hours during which telegraph
offices are open are from 8 a.m. to 8 p.m. ; Sundays, 8 a.m. to 10 a.m.

(t) Postal Districts with Telephone Communicaltion.

Any subscriber to a Telephone Exchange

may telephone messages to any post office connected with the telephone system in any other
area, for delivery as express letters, or for further transmission over the postal telegraphs and
delivery as telegrams. Public Call offices, or private lines to post offices, may also be used for

this purpose.

[In the following list of Post Offices where #wo places are given, the second is the Office from

which 1s served its correspondence.

When ¢kree places are given, the third is the nearest

Money Order Office; or in case the office is itself also a Money Order Office (*) the third
is that of the nearest Telegraph Office (#), with its distance from the post office, when it
exceeds three miles. No charge is made for delivery up to three miles ; porterage is calculated
from the boundary of area at the rate of 3d. per mile or part of a mile.]
